title.emphasis: Part III: Freedom of the Press

section.para: The First Amendment's explicit protection for "the freedom... of the press" recognizes the unique role of the institutional press in informing the public and holding the government accountable. The Supreme Court's jurisprudence in this area has established a powerful bulwark against government censorship, particularly through the doctrine against prior restraint, while also defining the limits of press rights in the context of newsgathering and the judicial process.

title.emphasis: A. The Presumption Against Prior Restraint

section.para: The Court has identified prior restraint—government action that prevents speech from being published in the first place—as the most serious and least tolerable infringement on First Amendment freedoms.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Near v. Minnesota **, 283 U.S. 697 (1931)**
section.para: This is the foundational case establishing the constitutional doctrine against prior restraint.74 A Minnesota law allowed public officials to seek an injunction to shut down any "malicious, scandalous and defamatory newspaper".76 County officials used the law to enjoin the publication of

The Saturday Press, a newspaper that had published articles accusing local officials of being implicated with gangsters.78 The Supreme Court, in a 5-4 decision, declared the Minnesota law unconstitutional.77 Chief Justice Charles Evans Hughes, writing for the majority, declared that the law was "the essence of censorship" and that the primary purpose of the press clause was to prevent such "previous restraints upon publication".76 While the Court acknowledged that prior restraint might be permissible in exceptional cases, such as to prevent the publication of troop movements in wartime, it established a heavy presumption against its constitutionality.77

section.title:
Case Analysis:
New York Times Co. v. United States **, 403 U.S. 713 (1971) (The Pentagon Papers Case)**
section.para: This case provided the ultimate test of the prior restraint doctrine. The Nixon administration sought to enjoin The New York Times and The Washington Post from publishing the Pentagon Papers, a classified Defense Department study on the history of U.S. activities in Vietnam, arguing that publication would cause "irreparable injury" to national security.79 In a landmark 6-3

per curiam decision, the Supreme Court held that the government had failed to meet its "heavy burden of showing justification for the enforcement of such a restraint".81 The Court's brief opinion was accompanied by nine separate concurring and dissenting opinions. The ruling powerfully affirmed that any system of prior restraints bears a heavy presumption against its constitutional validity and that the government must prove that publication would surely result in direct, immediate, and irreparable damage to the nation.80

title.emphasis: B. Newsgathering and the Judicial Process: Reporter's Privilege and Newsroom Searches

section.para: While the right to publish is strongly protected, the Court has been more circumspect about granting the press special rights in the newsgathering process, often holding that journalists are subject to the same legal obligations as other citizens.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Branzburg v. Hayes **, 408 U.S. 665 (1972)**
section.para: This case consolidated four cases in which reporters had been subpoenaed to testify before grand juries about confidential information and sources related to criminal activity they had observed.84 The reporters argued that forcing them to testify would compromise their ability to gather news by destroying the trust of confidential sources. In a 5-4 decision, the Supreme Court held that the First Amendment does not grant reporters a constitutional testimonial privilege to refuse to testify before a grand jury.84 The Court reasoned that the public interest in law enforcement and ensuring effective grand jury proceedings outweighed the "incidental burden" on newsgathering.85 In the wake of this decision, many states enacted "shield laws" to provide statutory protection for reporters' sources.84

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Zurcher v. Stanford Daily **, 436 U.S. 547 (1978)**
section.para: Police, seeking photographs to identify demonstrators who had assaulted officers at a hospital protest, obtained a warrant and searched the newsroom of The Stanford Daily, a student newspaper.86 The newspaper sued, arguing that newsrooms should be protected from such searches and that police should be required to use a subpoena

duces tecum instead of a warrant. The Supreme Court, in a 5-3 decision, ruled against the newspaper.87 The Court held that neither the First nor the Fourth Amendment forbids the issuance of a warrant to search for evidence simply because the owner of the premises is the press and is not suspected of a crime.86 The decision prompted significant outcry and led Congress to pass the Privacy Protection Act of 1980, which provides federal statutory protection against newsroom searches except in limited circumstances.87

title.emphasis: C. The Right to Publish Lawfully Obtained, Truthful Information

section.para: The Court has established a strong principle that the press cannot be punished for publishing truthful information that it has lawfully obtained, particularly when that information is a matter of public record or concern.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Cox Broadcasting Corp. v. Cohn **, 420 U.S. 469 (1975)**
section.para: A television station broadcast the name of a seventeen-year-old rape and murder victim, which a reporter had obtained from public court records.89 The victim's father sued for invasion of privacy, citing a Georgia statute that made it a misdemeanor to publicize the name of a rape victim.89 The Supreme Court held that the state could not impose sanctions on the accurate publication of a rape victim's name obtained from public records open to public inspection.90 The Court reasoned that the press has a responsibility to report on the operations of government, including judicial proceedings, and that the interests of privacy "fade" when information is already part of the public record.89

The Court extended the principle of Cox Broadcasting in this case. A newspaper, The Florida Star, published the full name of a rape victim, which a reporter-trainee had obtained from a police report that was inadvertently placed in the sheriff's department pressroom.92 The victim, B.J.F., sued and won damages under a Florida statute that made it illegal to publish a rape victim's name.92 The Supreme Court reversed the judgment, holding that "if a newspaper lawfully obtains truthful information about a matter of public significance then state officials may not constitutionally punish publication of the information, absent a need to further a state interest of the highest order".93 The Court found the Florida statute underinclusive because it punished only "instrument[s] of mass communication" and not individuals who might spread the information through gossip.92
